title: Data Quality Awareness
description: An awareness module on the importance of data quality for AI and organisational decision-making, covering the 15 data quality characteristics defined by ISO 25024, measurement approaches, data quality in the AI pipeline, and organisational responsibilities.

sections.title: Why Data Quality Matters for AI and Decision-Making
sections.content:
Data quality is a foundational concern for any organisation that relies on data to inform decisions, and it is of particular importance in the context of artificial intelligence. AI systems are inherently data-dependent; the quality of their outputs is directly influenced by the quality of the data used to train, validate, and operate them.

Poor data quality can lead to inaccurate predictions, biased outcomes, and unreliable analytics, undermining trust in both the AI system and the decisions it supports. In regulated industries, data quality failures may also result in non-compliance with legal and regulatory requirements, exposing the organisation to financial penalties and reputational harm.

Beyond AI, data quality affects the broader decision-making capability of an organisation. Strategic planning, operational management, and customer engagement all depend on the availability of accurate, complete, and timely data. When data quality is compromised, the organisation's ability to respond effectively to opportunities and threats is diminished.

ISO 25024 provides a structured framework for understanding and measuring data quality, enabling organisations to identify weaknesses, set improvement targets, and monitor progress over time. By investing in data quality, organisations strengthen the foundations upon which their AI systems and business processes are built.

sections.title: The 15 Data Quality Characteristics
sections.content:
ISO 25024 defines fifteen characteristics that together provide a comprehensive model for assessing data quality. These characteristics are grouped into categories that address different dimensions of quality, enabling organisations to evaluate data from multiple perspectives.

The characteristics include accuracy, completeness, consistency, credibility, and currentness, each of which addresses a distinct aspect of how well data represents the real-world entities and events it is intended to describe. Accuracy refers to the degree to which data correctly represents the true values, whilst completeness measures the extent to which all required data elements are present.

Consistency assesses whether data is free from contradictions and is coherent across different datasets and systems. Credibility evaluates the degree to which data is regarded as trustworthy and believable by its users. Currentness considers whether data is sufficiently up to date for the purposes for which it is being used.

Additional characteristics include accessibility, compliance, confidentiality, efficiency, precision, traceability, understandability, availability, portability, and recoverability. Together, these fifteen characteristics provide a robust basis for organisations to define, measure, and improve the quality of their data assets.

sections.title: Measuring Data Quality
sections.content:
Measuring data quality is essential for understanding the current state of an organisation's data assets and for identifying areas that require improvement. ISO 25024 provides guidance on defining data quality metrics and establishing measurement processes that are repeatable, transparent, and aligned with organisational objectives.

For each data quality characteristic, organisations should define specific metrics that quantify the degree to which the characteristic is met. For example, accuracy might be measured by comparing data values against a trusted reference source, whilst completeness could be assessed by calculating the proportion of required fields that contain valid entries.

Measurement should be conducted at defined intervals and at key points in the data lifecycle, such as during data acquisition, after data transformation, and before data is used for analysis or AI model training. Automated data quality tools can support efficient and consistent measurement, particularly for large and complex datasets.

The results of data quality measurement should be documented and communicated to relevant stakeholders, including data owners, data stewards, and users of the data. Visualisations such as dashboards and scorecards can help to convey the status of data quality in an accessible and actionable manner, supporting ongoing improvement efforts.

sections.title: Data Quality in the AI Pipeline
sections.content:
The AI pipeline — encompassing data collection, preprocessing, feature engineering, model training, validation, and deployment — presents numerous points at which data quality can be compromised. Ensuring data quality throughout this pipeline is critical to the performance, fairness, and reliability of AI systems.

At the data collection stage, organisations must ensure that data is sourced from reliable and representative sources, that consent and legal requirements are met, and that the data is sufficiently diverse to support the intended use case. Inadequate attention to data collection can introduce bias and limit the generalisability of the resulting model.

Preprocessing and feature engineering involve transforming raw data into a form suitable for model training. These steps can inadvertently introduce errors or remove important information if not conducted carefully. Organisations should establish clear procedures for data cleaning, normalisation, and feature selection, and should validate the quality of data at each transformation stage.

During model training and validation, data quality issues may manifest as poor model performance, unexpected behaviour, or failure to generalise to new data. Organisations should implement data quality checks as an integral part of the model development workflow, and should maintain traceability between the data used and the model outputs produced. This traceability supports auditability and facilitates the investigation of data quality-related incidents.

sections.title: Organisational Responsibilities for Data Quality
sections.content:
Achieving and maintaining high data quality requires clear organisational responsibilities and governance structures. ISO 25024 supports organisations in defining the roles, processes, and accountabilities needed to manage data quality effectively across the enterprise.

Data governance frameworks should establish the policies, standards, and procedures that guide data quality management. Key roles include data owners, who are accountable for the quality of specific data assets; data stewards, who are responsible for the day-to-day management and improvement of data quality; and data consumers, who have a responsibility to report data quality issues and to use data in accordance with established guidelines.

Senior management plays a critical role in championing data quality and ensuring that it receives appropriate attention and resources. Without visible leadership commitment, data quality initiatives are unlikely to achieve sustained impact. Management should set data quality objectives, allocate resources, and monitor progress against targets.

Training and awareness programmes are essential to building a data quality culture across the organisation. All staff who create, manage, or use data should understand the importance of data quality and their responsibilities in maintaining it. Regular communication of data quality metrics and improvement achievements helps to reinforce the message and to sustain engagement over time.

title: Data Quality Management & Measurement
description: A detailed module on managing and measuring data quality in accordance with ISO 25024, covering accuracy and completeness measurement, consistency and credibility assessment, currentness and timeliness, data quality improvement processes, and data quality reporting.

sections.title: Accuracy and Completeness Measurement
sections.content:
Accuracy and completeness are among the most fundamental data quality characteristics and are of particular importance for AI systems and analytical applications. Measuring these characteristics provides a direct indication of the reliability and usability of the data.

Accuracy measurement involves comparing data values against a reference source or established truth to determine the degree of correctness. This may be conducted through automated validation rules, manual sampling and review, or reconciliation with external data sources. The choice of method depends on the nature of the data, the availability of reference sources, and the level of precision required.

Completeness measurement assesses the extent to which all required data elements are present and populated with valid values. This includes checking for missing records, null or blank fields, and partially completed entries. Completeness metrics are typically expressed as a percentage of expected data elements that are present and valid.

Organisations should establish accuracy and completeness targets for each critical data asset, informed by the requirements of downstream processes and users. Regular measurement against these targets enables the identification of trends and the early detection of quality deterioration. Where measurement reveals deficiencies, root cause analysis should be conducted to determine the source of the problem and to inform corrective action.

sections.title: Consistency and Credibility Assessment
sections.content:
Consistency and credibility are essential characteristics for ensuring that data can be trusted and used effectively across the organisation. Consistency refers to the absence of contradictions within and between datasets, whilst credibility relates to the degree to which data is perceived as trustworthy by its users.

Assessing consistency involves checking that data values conform to defined rules and constraints, that related data elements are logically coherent, and that data is consistent across different systems and databases. For example, a customer's address should be the same in the sales system and the billing system. Inconsistencies may arise from data entry errors, integration failures, or differences in data definitions.

Credibility assessment considers the source of the data, the processes by which it was collected and maintained, and the track record of the data asset in terms of accuracy and reliability. Data from authoritative and well-managed sources is generally regarded as more credible than data from informal or unverified sources.

Organisations should implement automated checks and reconciliation processes to detect consistency issues and should establish criteria for assessing the credibility of data sources. Where inconsistencies or credibility concerns are identified, they should be logged, investigated, and resolved in a timely manner. Maintaining a record of data quality issues and their resolution supports transparency and continuous improvement.

sections.title: Currentness and Timeliness
sections.content:
Currentness and timeliness address whether data is sufficiently up to date and available when needed to support its intended use. In fast-moving environments and for AI systems that rely on real-time or near-real-time data, these characteristics are of critical importance.

Currentness measures the degree to which data reflects the most recent state of the real-world entities it represents. Data that was accurate at the time of collection may become outdated as circumstances change, leading to decisions based on obsolete information. Organisations should define currentness requirements for each data asset, taking account of the rate of change in the underlying domain and the sensitivity of downstream processes to data age.

Timeliness refers to the availability of data within the timeframe required for its intended use. Even if data is current, it may not be timely if it is not accessible to users and systems when needed. Timeliness is influenced by data processing schedules, system performance, and the efficiency of data delivery mechanisms.

Measuring currentness and timeliness involves tracking the age of data at the point of use, monitoring data refresh cycles, and assessing whether data is consistently available within defined service levels. Where gaps are identified, organisations should investigate the underlying causes — which may include delays in data capture, processing bottlenecks, or system outages — and implement measures to improve performance.

sections.title: Data Quality Improvement Processes
sections.content:
Data quality improvement is an ongoing process that requires systematic identification of quality issues, root cause analysis, and the implementation of targeted corrective and preventive actions. ISO 25024 supports organisations in establishing structured improvement processes that drive measurable and sustained gains in data quality.

The improvement process typically begins with the analysis of data quality measurement results to identify recurring issues, trends, and areas where quality falls below defined targets. Root cause analysis techniques, such as the five whys or fishbone diagrams, can be used to determine the underlying causes of quality problems and to distinguish between isolated incidents and systemic issues.

Corrective actions address the immediate quality issue — for example, cleansing or enriching affected data records — whilst preventive actions target the root cause to reduce the likelihood of recurrence. Preventive measures may include changes to data entry processes, improvements to system integrations, updates to validation rules, or enhancements to staff training.

Organisations should establish a continuous improvement cycle, incorporating regular review of data quality metrics, prioritisation of improvement initiatives, implementation of actions, and verification of their effectiveness. Engaging data owners, stewards, and users in the improvement process helps to ensure that efforts are focused on the areas of greatest impact and that improvements are sustained over time.

sections.title: Data Quality Reporting
sections.content:
Effective reporting is essential for communicating the status of data quality to stakeholders and for supporting informed decision-making about data management priorities and investments. Data quality reports should provide a clear and accurate picture of current quality levels, trends over time, and the outcomes of improvement initiatives.

Reports should be tailored to the needs of their audience. Senior management may require high-level summaries and trend information to support strategic decision-making, whilst data stewards and operational teams may need detailed breakdowns by data asset, characteristic, and time period. Dashboards and scorecards provide an accessible and visual means of presenting data quality information.

Key elements of a data quality report include the metrics measured, the current values against targets, trends over the reporting period, significant issues identified, actions taken, and planned improvements. Where data quality affects specific business processes or AI systems, the report should describe the impact and any associated risks.

Regular reporting cadence — whether weekly, monthly, or quarterly — should be established and adhered to, with additional reporting triggered by significant events or quality incidents. Transparency in reporting builds trust and accountability, and provides the evidence base needed to justify continued investment in data quality management. Organisations should also consider sharing relevant data quality information with external stakeholders, such as regulators and partners, where appropriate.
